items.q: What produces a magnetic field around a wire?
items.correct: A moving electric charge — i.e. an electric current.
items.misconception: The metal in the wire is naturally magnetic.
items.random_wrong: The voltage across the wire creates magnetism.
items.why: Students think magnetism is intrinsic to the wire material rather than a consequence of charge motion.

items.q: According to Faraday's law, what determines the size of the induced emf in a coil?
items.correct: The rate of change of magnetic flux linkage through the coil.
items.misconception: The strength of the magnetic field alone.
items.random_wrong: The number of turns in the coil alone.
items.why: Students focus on the field strength or the coil's properties in isolation and miss that it is the rate of change of flux linkage that matters.

items.q: What does the spacing of field lines on a diagram indicate?
items.correct: The strength of the field — closer lines mean a stronger field.
items.misconception: Field lines are equally important regardless of spacing — only the arrows matter.
items.random_wrong: Wider spacing means a stronger field because the lines have more room to act.
items.why: Students treat field lines as a count rather than a density-based representation; they miss that the convention encodes strength via spacing.

items.q: Wien's law allows us to determine a star's:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Surface temperature. “Distance” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
